description:
Today `repair_rebind::rebind_one` does four sequential `fs::rename` calls (worktrees, claims, locks, plugins-auth). If rename #N fails, renames 1..N-1 are already committed and the clone is half-moved — half its per-clone state at the new nested path, half at the old. The bl-05e5 implementation documents this as "atomic per move, not transactional," which is honest but leaves the user with no recovery path beyond "look at the mess and fix it by hand."

True POSIX atomicity across four independent renames is impossible — the four siblings have no common parent under SPEC §3's flat layout. This ball addresses the next-best thing: keep the layout, but on any sibling rename failure, reverse the renames that already succeeded so the clone lands back at the original nested path.

## Fix

In `src/repair_rebind.rs::rebind_one`:

1. Track `moved` (already exists) as the running list of (from, to) pairs that have committed.
2. When `fs::rename` fails on sibling N, iterate `moved` in reverse and `fs::rename(to, from)` to undo. Collect any reverse-rename errors; do not abort the reversal on a single failure (a half-reversed half-moved state is worse than a fully-moved one).
3. Compose the surfaced error to name both ends: the original failure AND any reverse-rename failures. The CLI presents the user with the full picture: "rename A → B failed: <reason>; reverse-rolled C → D, E → F; reverse FAILED for G → H: <reason>".
4. Do NOT rewrite the breadcrumb at the new location on failure — the recorded path must continue to record wherever the data actually sits. On successful reversal, the breadcrumb at the OLD location still records the original clone path; on partial reversal failure, the breadcrumb at the partially-new location records the user-intended new path AND the surfaced error names both ends so the user can rebind by hand.

## Why this is a separate ball from bl-f8f3 (detection bug)

bl-f8f3 fixes a logic inversion in `find_orphans`; the rebind action itself is correct against the (corrected) detection. Reverse-on-failure is a behaviour change in the action verb, not the detection. They touch the same files but are independently reviewable and independently shippable. bl-f8f3 is the dependency — there is no value reversing a rebind that was never correctly triggered.

## Why not the SPEC change (per-clone parent dir)

The genuinely-atomic alternative would change SPEC §3 to nest all four siblings under `per-clone/<nested>/`, making the rebind one rename. That is the principled answer and stays an option for a future SPEC revision, but it is a layout pivot mid-epic (bl-f021 is the layout pivot already in flight). Reverse-on-failure inside the current layout gets us recoverability without re-touching the SPEC.

## Conformance

- A rebind whose third sibling rename fails leaves all four siblings at the original nested path. The surfaced error names the rename that failed AND lists the rolled-back siblings.
- A rebind whose third sibling rename fails AND whose reverse-rename also fails surfaces both failures and leaves the data wherever it actually sits — no silent corruption.
- A successful rebind (no failures) behaves identically to today: four renames, breadcrumb rewritten at the new location.
title: bl repair --rebind-path: reverse-on-failure for partial rename failures
